source.definition: to give a name to something or to designate it

explanation.sourceText: The verb 'name' comes from the Old English 'nama', which means 'a name' or 'to give a name'. It is related to similar words in other Germanic languages and ultimately derives from the Proto-Indo-European root *h₁nómn̥.
explanation.targetText: Verbul „name“ provine din engleza veche „nama“, care înseamnă „un nume“ sau „a da un nume“. Este legat de cuvinte similare din alte limbi germanice și derivă în cele din urmă din rădăcina proto-indo-europeană *h₁nómn̥.

source.additional: Regular verb. Commonly used when assigning names to people, places, or things.

target.definition: a da un nume sau a desemna ceva

explanation.sourceText: The Romanian verb „a numi“ derives from the Latin „nominare“, meaning "to give a name" or "to name". It is closely connected with identification and classification.
explanation.targetText: Verbul românesc „a numi“ derivă din latinescul „nominare“, care înseamnă „a da un nume“ sau „a numi“.

target.additional: Regular verb of the 1st conjugation (-a). Frequently used in expressions like: „Eu numesc pe el“ (I name him).

examples.sourceText: Do you name the new boss?
They named the streets after famous personalities.
examples.targetText: Ei numeau străzile după personalități cunoscute.

examples.sourceText: The named person in charge is absent.

learningTips.text: Remember the Romanian verb 'a numi' by linking it to the English word 'name'—both relate to giving names.
Think of 'numi' sounding like 'namey'—a playful way to recall it means 'to name'.
Imagine introducing your friends: 'Eu numesc prietenii mei' means 'I name my friends', helping you connect the verb with naming.
The root 'num-' appears in English words like 'numerous' and 'number', which can help you remember 'a numi' relates to naming or numbering.
